content.text: A value realization assumption is any premise in a business case that converts an observed capability into a projected financial benefit. The phrase 'Claude saves 33 minutes per contract review' is a measured fact. The phrase 'those 33 minutes translate to $36,960 per year in recoverable value' is a value realization assumption — it depends on how many contracts are reviewed, what the role costs per hour, and how much of the saved time is actually reinvested in productive work.

Sensitivity analysis quantifies how much the projected ROI changes when each assumption moves from its base value to the edge of its plausible range. Together, value realization assumptions and sensitivity analysis form the analytical core of a credible business case — replacing a single optimistic projection with a structured view of uncertainty.

A business case that presents a single ROI number without sensitivity analysis is not presenting a projection — it is presenting a best case dressed as an expectation. Finance reviewers know this. Building in the sensitivity analysis proactively signals analytical rigour and earns more trust than the single-number alternative.
The Five Core Value Realization Assumptions
Across the range of Claude enterprise use cases, five assumptions recur as the primary drivers of whether projected benefits are realised:

The realization rate (also called the productivity capture rate) is the fraction of saved time that is actually reinvested in productive work rather than absorbed by meetings, context-switching, idle time, or new administrative load. If Claude saves a paralegal 33 minutes per contract, and the paralegal spends 20 of those minutes in a status meeting, 13 minutes of productive capacity is recovered — a realization rate of 39%, not the 70% assumed in the base case.
Standard realization rate values by context:
Saved time goes directly into more of the same type of work; clear reinvestment path
Saved time competes with other task types; reinvestment requires active discipline
Saved time often absorbed by meetings, email, and decision-making that expands to fill available time
Volume absorbs capacity gains; teams process more transactions, not fewer hours
Time savings rarely translate to measurable output differences at this level

The realization rate is the most frequently challenged assumption in finance reviews — and the most frequently overstated in initial business cases. Using 0.70 as the default without justification will be questioned. Being able to explain why you chose 0.70 (or adjusting to 0.60 as a conservative default) is the difference between a credible and an optimistic case.

The volume assumption is the projected number of times the use case will be invoked per month (or year). Volume drives both cost (more calls = more token cost) and benefit (more tasks processed = more time saved). It is the assumption with the highest sensitivity in most ROI models.
Volume assumptions must be sourced — not estimated. Acceptable sources:

Pull the actual count of the task from the system that currently tracks it (CRM, ticketing system, document management system). A 'contract review' count sourced from the legal team's matter management system is defensible; an estimate from the General Counsel is not.

For tasks that are not tracked in any system, measure the actual volume over a representative 4-week period and annualise. A 4-week sample is the minimum; 8–12 weeks is better for tasks with seasonal variation.
Conservative multiplier on known adjacent volume:
If contracts processed is not tracked but invoices processed is, estimate contracts as a fraction of invoices using a documented assumption.
The baseline is the time (or error rate, or cost) of performing the task without Claude. The baseline is not an estimate — it must be measured. The two measurement approaches:

Ask 5–10 practitioners to time themselves on the target task for 10–20 instances each. Record start and end time per instance. Use the median (not the mean) to reduce the impact of outliers. A task that 'takes about 30 minutes' typically measures at 48–55 minutes in a time-and-motion study.

If the task has digital start and end events logged (a ticket opened and closed, a document checked in and out), the delta is the task duration. Adjust for tasks that span multiple sessions (a contract review that starts Monday and finishes Thursday requires adjustment for working hours only).

The baseline measurement must cover the same task scope as the Claude deployment. If Claude handles only the initial drafting phase of contract review (not the legal judgement and negotiation), the baseline should measure only the drafting phase, not the end-to-end review time.

Time savings are valued at the fully-loaded hourly cost of the role performing the task — not the salary. The fully-loaded rate includes employer taxes, benefits, office overhead, IT allocation, and management overhead. For most enterprise roles, the fully-loaded rate is 1.3–1.7× the base salary.

Use the fully-loaded rate for all benefit calculations. Using only salary understates the benefit by 30–50% and may cause a borderline use case to fail a business case when it would actually clear the bar. Finance teams will accept the fully-loaded rate when it is documented — the multiplier is standard accounting practice.

The adoption assumption is the fraction of the target user population that will actively use Claude for the task within the first 12 months. An adoption rate of 80% means 4 out of 5 target users have incorporated Claude into their workflow by month 12; a rate of 40% means fewer than half have.
Adoption is frequently underestimated in initial projections and is the primary cause of first-year benefit shortfalls. It is driven by:
Ease of access (whether Claude is integrated into existing tools or requires a separate login)
Manager reinforcement (whether team leads encourage use)
Output quality (whether early users find Claude's output genuinely saves them time)
Conservative adoption rate assumptions by deployment model:
Embedded in existing tool (e.g. CRM integration)

Workflow-mandatory deployments — where Claude is embedded in a required step in a business process — achieve near-100% adoption but sacrifice the voluntary quality signal. High mandatory adoption with poor output quality produces high adoption and low realization, because users complete the Claude step and then redo the work manually.

attrs.alt: Value realization pathway from deployment to confirmed benefit

content.text: Sensitivity analysis holds all assumptions at their base values except one, varies that one assumption across its plausible range, and records the resulting change in the outcome metric (typically Year 1 net benefit or ROI %). Repeating this for each assumption produces a ranked list of which assumptions matter most — which drives the tornado chart.

attrs.alt: Tornado chart showing sensitivity of Year 1 net benefit to key assumptions

content.text:
# Sensitivity analysis for a Claude ROI model
def base_net_benefit(
    annual_volume=1_440,          # contracts/year
    time_saved_per_task_hrs=0.55, # hours saved per contract
    hourly_rate=120,              # fully-loaded $/hr
    realization_rate=0.70,
    integration_cost=18_000,
    annual_ops_cost=9_600,
    annual_token_cost=105,
):
    total_cost = integration_cost + annual_ops_cost + annual_token_cost
    total_benefit = annual_volume * time_saved_per_task_hrs * hourly_rate * realization_rate
    return total_benefit - total_cost

# Tornado: vary each assumption independently between conservative and optimistic
assumptions = {
    "Annual Volume":      {"conservative": 600,  "base": 1_440,  "optimistic": 3_240},
    "Time Saved/Task":    {"conservative": 0.25, "base": 0.55,   "optimistic": 0.60},
    "Realization Rate":   {"conservative": 0.50, "base": 0.70,   "optimistic": 0.90},
    "Hourly Rate":        {"conservative": 80,   "base": 120,    "optimistic": 160},
    "Integration Cost":   {"conservative": 12_000, "base": 18_000, "optimistic": 24_000},
    "Annual Token Cost":  {"conservative": 21,   "base": 105,    "optimistic": 427},
}

base = base_net_benefit()
print(f"Base net benefit: ${base:,.0f}\n")
print(f"{'Assumption':<22} {'Conservative':>14} {'Base':>10} {'Optimistic':>12} {'Range':>10}")
print("-" * 75)

results = []
for name, vals in assumptions.items():
    kwargs = {}
    # Map display name to function argument
    arg_map = {
        "Annual Volume": "annual_volume",
        "Time Saved/Task": "time_saved_per_task_hrs",
        "Realization Rate": "realization_rate",
        "Hourly Rate": "hourly_rate",
        "Integration Cost": "integration_cost",
        "Annual Token Cost": "annual_token_cost",
    }
    arg = arg_map[name]

    conservative_val = base_net_benefit(**{arg: vals["conservative"]})
    optimistic_val = base_net_benefit(**{arg: vals["optimistic"]})
    rng = abs(optimistic_val - conservative_val)
    results.append((rng, name, conservative_val, optimistic_val))

results.sort(reverse=True)  # tornado order: highest range at top
for rng, name, cons, opt in results:
    print(f"{name:<22} ${cons:>12,.0f} ${base:>8,.0f} ${opt:>10,.0f} ${rng:>8,.0f}")

# Assumption          Conservative       Base   Optimistic      Range
# Annual Volume           $6,321    $44,871    $80,193    $73,872  ← highest sensitivity
# Time Saved/Task        $17,721    $44,871    $69,321    $51,600
# Realization Rate       $27,921    $44,871    $61,821    $33,900
# Hourly Rate            $33,471    $44,871    $56,271    $22,800
# Integration Cost       $50,871    $44,871    $38,871    $12,000
# Annual Token Cost      $44,955    $44,871    $44,549       $406  ← lowest sensitivity

The output ranks assumptions by their impact range. Volume is the most sensitive assumption — the difference between the conservative and optimistic volume projections spans $73,872 of net benefit. Token cost is the least sensitive — varying from Haiku to Sonnet to Opus changes the net benefit by only $406. This confirms that the model selection conversation should happen once (choose the cheapest model that meets quality requirements) and the volume conversation should happen continuously.

A well-managed ROI model maintains an assumption register — a structured record of every value realization assumption, its source, the base value, and the conservative and optimistic bounds. The register serves as the audit trail for the business case and as the basis for the 90-day and 12-month post-launch reviews.

# Value Realization Assumption Register
# Use Case: Contract Review Assistant
# Last Updated: [Date]
# Reviewed By: [Finance Liaison Name]

| Assumption          | Base Value     | Conservative   | Optimistic     | Source                              | Owner        |
|---------------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|-------------------------------------|--------------|
| Annual Volume       | 1,440 contracts| 600            | 3,240          | Matter mgmt system, 12-month pull   | Legal Ops    |
| Time Saved/Task     | 33 min (0.55h) | 15 min (0.25h) | 36 min (0.60h) | Time-and-motion study, n=25, 10 tasks each | Programme team |
| Realization Rate    | 0.70           | 0.50           | 0.90           | Standard enterprise default; embedded tool | Finance      |
| Hourly Rate         | $120           | $80            | $160           | HR: avg fully-loaded rate, paralegal grade | HR Finance   |
| Integration Cost    | $18,000        | $12,000        | $24,000        | Engineering estimate, ±33% range    | Platform Eng |
| Annual Token Cost   | $105           | $21 (Haiku)    | $427 (Sonnet+RAG) | Token model, Sonnet base case    | Platform Eng |
| Adoption Rate (M12) | 70%            | 40%            | 90%            | Embedded tool in existing workflow  | Change Mgmt  |

## Assumption Change Log
| Date | Assumption | Old Value | New Value | Reason |
|------|------------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| [Q2 90-day review] | Annual Volume | 1,440 | 1,200 | Actual M3 volume was 85/month, not 120 projected |

The most effective presentation format for sensitivity analysis in a business case is the three-scenario table followed by the tornado chart (for technical audiences) or the scenario narrative (for executive audiences).

Present conservative, base, and optimistic scenarios side by side. For each, show total cost, total benefit, net benefit, and ROI. The table should make it immediately obvious which scenario the organisation is most likely in.

When presenting to a CFO: 'In our conservative scenario, we do not recover the integration cost in Year 1 — but in the base and optimistic scenarios, we pay back in under 6 months. The conservative scenario is driven entirely by volume; if the Legal Ops team can confirm 80+ contracts per month, the conservative case disappears and we are in base-case territory.'

For executive presentations, pair the three-scenario table with a one-paragraph narrative for each scenario that describes the conditions under which that scenario occurs — not the numbers, but the world in which the numbers are true:

'If Legal Ops processes fewer than 60 contracts per month, or if the team finds that Claude's drafts require significant rewriting (more than 20 minutes of editing), the deployment does not pay back in Year 1. This is most likely if the pilot reveals quality issues in the specialised contract clauses handled by the Corporate Finance team, or if the Q4 M&A slowdown reduces contract volume below the projected rate.'

'With 120 contracts per month and 33-minute average time savings, the deployment pays back in approximately 5.5 months and generates $44,871 net in Year 1. This is the expected scenario given current Legal Ops volume and the pilot quality results we observed on the 50-contract test set.'

'If Legal Ops volumes grow with the planned expansion into the APAC region (which would add 60–80 contracts per month by Q3), and if the team achieves 90% realization of time savings through manager reinforcement, Year 1 net benefit exceeds $158,000. This scenario requires both the APAC expansion to proceed on schedule and the change management programme to achieve high adoption.'
Common Assumption Errors and How to Catch Them
Even experienced programme teams make systematic errors in value realization assumptions. Knowing the patterns makes them avoidable:

The baseline measurement covers the full task (e.g., end-to-end contract review: drafting + negotiation + legal sign-off = 3 hours), but Claude only handles a portion (drafting = 45 minutes). The ROI model applies the full task saving to Claude's scope, inflating the projected benefit by 3–4×.

Fix: explicitly scope the baseline to match Claude's intervention boundary. If Claude drafts the first-pass summary, measure only the drafting time — not the time to finalise and execute the contract.

The volume figure (e.g., 1,200 contracts/year) is the total corporate volume. But only one team (Legal Ops, 12 people) is in scope for the deployment. The actual target population processes 200 contracts/year. The model overstates volume by 6×.
Fix: source volume from the specific team or system scope of the deployment, not the enterprise total. When multiple teams are in scope, list them individually and sum their volumes explicitly.

The model assumes that Claude's output is used as-is (or with minor edits), producing the full 33-minute saving. In practice, users treat Claude's output as a first draft that requires significant editing — reducing the effective saving to 10–15 minutes.

The pilot must measure actual time-with-Claude (including editing time), not just the time Claude takes to generate output. The saving is: (baseline task time) minus (time to prompt Claude + time to review and edit output). Omitting the review and edit time overstates the saving by 20–50% for drafting use cases.

The model assumes the time saving is permanent across the projection period. In practice, task volume and complexity may change, user proficiency may plateau, and the underlying process may evolve. A use case that saves 33 minutes per contract when contracts average 15 pages will save less if average contract length grows to 40 pages (Claude still helps, but the user review time scales with complexity).

Fix: model a proficiency ramp for the first 6 months (50% of the base saving in M1–M3, 75% in M4–M6, 100% from M7 onwards) and flag in the assumption register that a re-measurement is required at the 12-month mark.

The optimistic scenario applies the optimistic value to every assumption simultaneously (optimistic volume AND optimistic saving per task AND optimistic realization rate AND optimistic adoption). This is not a coherent scenario — it is an upper bound on the model, not a plausible outcome.

Coherent scenarios: the optimistic scenario should be the outcome if the primary driver (usually volume) comes in at the top of its range, with other assumptions at base. The conservative scenario should be the outcome if the primary driver comes in at the bottom of its range. Only vary all assumptions together for the mathematical bounds table — present it clearly as a range, not a scenario.
The Ramp-Up Period in Benefit Projections

Benefits do not materialise on day one of deployment. There is a ramp-up period during which users learn to use Claude, develop workflow discipline, and reach the full realization rate. Failing to model the ramp-up overstates Year 1 benefits by 15–30% in typical deployments.
A standard ramp-up curve for a well-executed deployment:
Applying this ramp-up to the Year 1 benefit calculation:

# Ramp-up adjusted Year 1 benefit calculation
monthly_benefits = []
base_monthly_benefit = 1_440 * 0.55 * 120 * 0.70 / 12  # $6,930/month at full steady-state

ramp_multipliers = [0.10, 0.20, 0.33, 0.50, 0.50, 0.50, 0.65, 0.65, 0.70, 0.70, 0.70, 0.70]

for month, multiplier in enumerate(ramp_multipliers, 1):
    benefit = base_monthly_benefit * multiplier
    monthly_benefits.append(benefit)
    print(f"Month {month:2d}: ${benefit:,.0f}")

print(f"\nYear 1 Total (ramp-adjusted): ${sum(monthly_benefits):,.0f}")
print(f"Year 1 Total (no ramp):        ${base_monthly_benefit * 12:,.0f}")
print(f"Ramp effect:                   {(1 - sum(monthly_benefits)/(base_monthly_benefit*12))*100:.0f}% reduction")

# Month  1: $693
# Month  2: $1,386
# Month  3: $2,287
# Month  4: $3,465
# ...
# Month 12: $4,851
#
# Year 1 Total (ramp-adjusted): $34,020
# Year 1 Total (no ramp):        $83,160
# Ramp effect:                   59% reduction

The ramp effect can reduce Year 1 benefit by 40–60% relative to a flat assumption. This is why Year 1 ROI almost always looks worse than Year 2 and Year 3 — not because the deployment is underperforming, but because full value realisation takes 6–9 months to reach. Present the ramp curve alongside the three-scenario table so decision-makers understand this is expected, not a sign of failure.
Validating Assumptions in the Pilot Phase

The pilot phase — typically 4–8 weeks with a small group of early adopters — is the primary mechanism for validating value realization assumptions before committing to full deployment. A well-designed pilot directly tests the three highest-sensitivity assumptions:
Testing the Time Saving (Baseline vs With-Claude)

During the pilot, measure both the baseline task time (completed without Claude, by a control group) and the with-Claude task time (completed with Claude, by the pilot group). Use the same measurement methodology as the baseline study: timed from task start to first complete draft, not from task start to final submission.
For time savings: minimum 30 instances per group (pilot and control) to achieve statistical significance on a mean difference
For error rate reduction: minimum 50 instances per group — error rates are lower-frequency events that require larger samples to detect
For adoption: track weekly active users vs total licensed users across the pilot period — you need at least 6 weeks to observe the adoption trajectory

Realization is the hardest assumption to test directly during a pilot, because it requires observing what users do with the time they save — which is difficult to measure without intrusive monitoring. Two proxy approaches:

If the pilot group produces more output (more contracts reviewed, more analyses completed) than the control group in the same time period, realization is occurring. Measure output volume per person-hour for both groups.
Ask pilot users to log what they did with time they attribute to Claude. Even a 1-week diary study for 10 users provides useful signal on whether saved time is being reinvested or absorbed.
After the pilot, update the assumption register with pilot-measured values:
Replace the estimated time saving with the measured pilot saving (and document the sample size and confidence interval)
Replace the adoption rate assumption with the measured pilot adoption rate at week 4 and week 8
Update the volume assumption if the pilot revealed that the original source was incorrect
Document any new assumptions that the pilot revealed (e.g., 'the review step takes 8 minutes per contract rather than the 5 minutes originally estimated')

A business case that has been updated with pilot measurements is substantially more credible than one built entirely on estimates. Present the pre-pilot and post-pilot models side by side if the update materially changes the ROI; acknowledge the change transparently rather than quietly updating the numbers.
When Sensitivity Analysis Reveals a Weak Case

Sometimes the sensitivity analysis reveals that the business case only works under optimistic assumptions — that the conservative scenario is negative and the base case is borderline. This is not a failure of the analysis; it is exactly what the analysis is designed to surface.
When the case is weak under conservative assumptions, the programme team has four options:

Can the integration be simplified to reduce the one-time cost? Can a larger population be included to increase volume? Can the deployment be extended to a higher-value task where the time saving per instance is larger?

Make the business case conditional on a volume commitment from the BU owner. 'We will proceed if Legal Ops commits to routing a minimum of 80 contracts per month through the system in Year 1.' This converts a borderline unconditional case into a conditional case with a stronger expected value.

Rather than a full production deployment, propose a limited pilot that tests the key sensitivity assumptions before committing the full integration cost. The pilot cost is typically $2,000–$5,000; confirming that the volume and time saving assumptions hold before spending $18,000 on the integration is good risk management.

If none of the above works, document the use case in the backlog with the sensitivity analysis attached and a note on what conditions would change the economics. Revisit when volume grows, when the role cost increases, or when a simpler integration path becomes available.

Deferring a weak case is not failure — it is programme discipline. A programme that funds weak cases to fill deployment slots will accumulate a portfolio of underperforming use cases and lose credibility with finance and leadership. A programme that funds only cases that clear the bar in the base scenario, and documents why deferred cases were deferred, builds the credibility to fund the harder cases later.

Value realization assumptions are the premises that connect a Claude capability to a projected financial benefit. The five core assumptions — realization rate, volume, baseline measurement, hourly rate, and adoption — each drive the ROI model significantly and must be sourced, not estimated. Sensitivity analysis ranks these assumptions by their impact on the outcome, producing a tornado chart that tells the team where to focus their verification effort.

The assumption register documents every premise with its source, owner, and plausible range, providing both the business case audit trail and the measurement baseline for the 90-day and 12-month post-launch reviews. Presenting three scenarios rather than a single point estimate replaces a best-case dressed as an expectation with a structured view of uncertainty — and earns significantly more credibility with finance and executive audiences who have seen optimistic AI projections fail to materialise.
